abstract: This paper describes the development of a gold(I)-catalyzed sulfination of aryl boronic acids, a transformation that leverages the fundamental organometallic reactivity of gold(I)–heteroatom bonds. Unlike traditional gold catalysis, which typically focuses on $\pi$-bond activation, this method utilizes an unprecedented redox-neutral cycle to generate sulfinate anions via sulfur dioxide insertion. These sulfinate intermediates can be elaborated in situ to produce a variety of sulfones and sulfonamides—two critical pharmacophores frequently targeted in medicinal chemistry. The utility of this approach is further demonstrated through the efficient assembly of targeted molecular libraries based on the indazole framework, offering a robust and divergent synthetic route for drug discovery and structure-activity relationship studies.
